explanation: In serial transmission, bits are sent one at a time over a single communication channel, one after another in sequence.

text: What does the term 'propagation delay' refer to in networking?
options: Time to encrypt and decrypt data packets
Time to convert data between formats
Time to process packets inside a router
Time for a signal to travel from source to destination
explanation: Propagation delay is the time it takes for a signal to travel from the sender to the receiver across the transmission medium.

text: Which signal type uses discrete voltage levels to represent data?
options: Acoustic signal through air medium
Analog signal with continuous waves
Digital signal with distinct levels
Modulated carrier frequency signal
explanation: A digital signal uses discrete (distinct) voltage levels to represent data, typically using two levels for binary representation of 0s and 1s.

text: What is the relationship between bit rate and baud rate in QPSK modulation?
options: Bit rate is twice the baud rate value
Bit rate equals the baud rate exactly
Bit rate is four times the baud rate
Bit rate is half the baud rate value
explanation: In QPSK (Quadrature Phase Shift Keying), each symbol represents 2 bits, so the bit rate is twice the baud rate.

text: What is the primary purpose of line coding in digital communication?
options: To multiplex several analog voice channels
To convert digital data into digital signals
To compress data before wireless transmission
To encrypt data for secure communication
explanation: Line coding converts a sequence of digital data (bits) into a digital signal suitable for transmission over a physical medium.

text: In Frequency Division Multiplexing, what prevents interference between channels?
options: Time slots assigned to each channel
Unique code sequences per channel
Guard bands separating frequency ranges
Priority levels for each data stream
explanation: Guard bands are unused frequency ranges placed between adjacent channels in FDM to prevent interference and signal overlap.

text: What is the advantage of NRZ-I encoding over NRZ-L encoding?
options: NRZ-I doubles the effective data rate
NRZ-I uses less bandwidth per signal
NRZ-I eliminates all baseline wander
NRZ-I provides better clock synchronization
explanation: NRZ-I (Non-Return-to-Zero Inverted) provides better clock synchronization than NRZ-L because transitions at bit boundaries help the receiver maintain timing.

text: A channel has a bandwidth of 6 kHz. Using the Nyquist formula with 4 signal levels, what is the maximum data rate?
options: 24 kbps with 4 levels encoding
48 kbps with 4 levels encoding
36 kbps with 4 levels encoding
12 kbps with 4 levels encoding
explanation: Using Nyquist formula: Data Rate = 2 x Bandwidth x log2(L) = 2 x 6000 x log2(4) = 2 x 6000 x 2 = 24,000 bps or 24 kbps.

text: What is the primary difference between baseband and broadband transmission?
options: Broadband uses digital signals on the entire bandwidth while baseband divides it
Baseband uses digital signals on the entire bandwidth while broadband divides it
Baseband supports higher data rates than broadband transmission systems
Broadband can only carry a single channel at any given time
explanation: Baseband transmission uses the entire bandwidth of the medium for a single digital signal, while broadband divides the bandwidth into multiple frequency channels for simultaneous transmissions.

text: What problem does scrambling solve in data communication systems?
options: It compresses data for faster throughput
It increases signal power for long cables
It reduces latency in fiber optic networks
It avoids long sequences of constant bits
explanation: Scrambling replaces long sequences of constant bits (like consecutive zeros) with patterns that ensure frequent signal transitions, aiding clock synchronization.

text: In Phase Shift Keying (PSK), what property of the carrier signal is modified?
options: The phase of the carrier wave signal
The frequency of the carrier wave
The wavelength of the carrier wave
The amplitude of the carrier wave
explanation: In PSK, the phase of the carrier signal is changed to represent different data values while the amplitude and frequency remain constant.

text: What is the role of an equalizer in a data communication receiver?
options: To amplify signals for longer distances
To compensate for signal distortion caused by the channel
To encrypt incoming data for secure storage
To convert analog signals into digital format
explanation: An equalizer compensates for frequency-dependent signal distortion (like intersymbol interference) introduced by the communication channel.

text: In Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK), how are binary values represented?
options: By changing the frequency between two values
By shifting the phase at each bit boundary
By varying the amplitude of the carrier wave
By combining frequency and phase variations
explanation: In ASK, binary data is represented by varying the amplitude of the carrier signal, typically using one amplitude for 1 and another (or zero) for 0.

text: In a 256-QAM modulation scheme, how many bits does each symbol carry?
options: 10 bits per symbol in the constellation
8 bits per symbol in the constellation
12 bits per symbol in the constellation
6 bits per symbol in the constellation
explanation: 256-QAM has 256 signal points, and log2(256) = 8, so each symbol represents 8 bits of data.

text: A noisy channel has a bandwidth of 3 kHz and SNR of 127. What is the Shannon capacity?
options: 35 kbps maximum capacity
28 kbps maximum capacity
14 kbps maximum capacity
21 kbps maximum capacity
explanation: Shannon capacity = B x log2(1 + SNR) = 3000 x log2(128) = 3000 x 7 = 21,000 bps or 21 kbps.

text: What is the fundamental problem with unipolar NRZ encoding for long data streams?
options: It consumes excessive bandwidth per symbol
It cannot operate above 1 Mbps data rates
It introduces a DC component causing baseline wander
It requires complex decoding circuitry at receivers
explanation: Unipolar NRZ creates a non-zero DC component because the average signal level is not zero, causing baseline wander that makes it difficult for the receiver to distinguish between levels.

text: In Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS), what determines the spreading factor?
options: The ratio of chip rate to data bit rate in the system
The number of time slots available in each frame period
The amplitude variation range of the modulated signal
The number of frequency hops per second in the pattern
explanation: The spreading factor in DSSS is the ratio of the chip rate to the data bit rate. A higher spreading factor provides more noise resistance but requires more bandwidth.

text: Why is the Shannon limit considered a theoretical upper bound for channel capacity?
options: It assumes error-free coding with infinite complexity at the encoder
It assumes zero latency in all signal processing hardware stages
It assumes synchronous clocking across all connected network nodes
It assumes ideal filtering with zero guard bands between channels
explanation: The Shannon limit is theoretical because achieving it would require infinitely complex coding schemes. Practical systems operate below this limit due to real-world coding constraints.

text: What distinguishes Differential Phase Shift Keying (DPSK) from conventional PSK?
options: DPSK requires a coherent carrier reference at the receiver
DPSK uses amplitude changes instead of phase changes
DPSK operates at double the frequency of conventional PSK
DPSK encodes data as changes between consecutive phase values
explanation: DPSK encodes data in the phase difference between consecutive symbols rather than absolute phase values, eliminating the need for a coherent reference carrier at the receiver.

text: What is the Nyquist bandwidth for a channel carrying a signal with a baud rate of 10,000 symbols/s?
options: 10 kHz minimum channel bandwidth
5 kHz minimum channel bandwidth
20 kHz minimum channel bandwidth
15 kHz minimum channel bandwidth
explanation: According to the Nyquist theorem, the minimum bandwidth needed is half the baud rate: B = Baud Rate / 2 = 10,000 / 2 = 5,000 Hz or 5 kHz.

text: In CDMA, what happens when a receiver multiplies a received composite signal by a station's chip sequence?
options: It amplifies signals from all stations equally for decoding
It compresses the composite signal to reduce overall bandwidth
It filters out the target station's data by orthogonal separation
It encrypts the signal with the station's unique security key
explanation: When the receiver multiplies the composite signal by a specific station's chip sequence, the orthogonal property of chip codes causes other stations' data to cancel out, extracting only the target data.

text: What is the main advantage of OFDM over single-carrier modulation in wideband channels?
options: OFDM eliminates the need for error correction coding entirely
OFDM is resilient to multipath fading by using parallel subcarriers
OFDM doubles the available spectrum through signal compression
OFDM reduces transmitter power consumption significantly
explanation: OFDM divides a wideband channel into many narrow subcarriers, each experiencing flat fading rather than frequency-selective fading, making it highly resilient to multipath propagation.

text: A PCM system samples a signal at 16,000 samples/s using 12-bit quantization. What is the resulting data rate?
options: 128 kbps output data stream
224 kbps output data stream
192 kbps output data stream
160 kbps output data stream
explanation: The data rate of a PCM system is calculated as: Sampling Rate x Bits per Sample = 16,000 x 12 = 192,000 bps or 192 kbps.

text: What is the primary function of a network interface card (NIC)?
options: To connect a device to a computer network
To store data temporarily during transfers
To route packets between different networks
To provide wireless power to portable devices
explanation: A Network Interface Card (NIC) provides the hardware interface that connects a computer or device to a network, enabling it to send and receive data.

text: Which network topology arranges all devices in a circular chain?
options: Mesh topology with full connections
Bus topology with shared backbone
Star topology with central device
Ring topology with circular connection
explanation: In a ring topology, each device is connected to exactly two other devices, forming a circular pathway for data transmission.

text: What is a Metropolitan Area Network (MAN)?
options: A network connecting countries across continents
A network spanning a single room or building floor
A personal network within a few meters range
A network covering a city or large campus area
explanation: A MAN covers a metropolitan area such as a city or large campus, larger than a LAN but smaller than a WAN.

text: What does a gateway do in computer networking?
options: It stores web pages for faster user access
It connects networks using different protocols
It monitors bandwidth usage on local links
It amplifies signals on a single network
explanation: A gateway connects two networks that may use different protocols, performing protocol conversion to enable communication between them.

text: Which layer of the OSI model is responsible for data formatting and encryption?
options: The session layer managing connections
The transport layer handling segments
The presentation layer handling format
The application layer for user access
explanation: The presentation layer (Layer 6) is responsible for data formatting, encryption, compression, and translation between different data representations.

text: What is a peer-to-peer (P2P) network model?
options: A model where each device can act as client and server
A model requiring a central database for all data
A model where one server manages all resources
A model using only wireless communication links
explanation: In a peer-to-peer network, each device can function as both a client and a server, sharing resources directly without a dedicated central server.

text: What does bandwidth refer to in computer networking?
options: The total storage available on a file server
The maximum data transfer capacity of a link
The number of devices connected to a network
The physical length of a network cable
explanation: Bandwidth in networking refers to the maximum rate at which data can be transferred over a network link, typically measured in bits per second.

text: What is the client-server model in networking?
options: A model where data is stored exclusively on client machines
A model where devices share resources equally without hierarchy
A model where clients request services from a dedicated server
A model where only one device is connected at any given time
explanation: In the client-server model, client devices request services or resources from a central server that manages and provides those services.

text: What does the physical layer of the OSI model deal with?
options: Logical addressing and routing decisions
Session establishment between applications
Error detection within transmitted frames
Raw bit transmission over physical media
explanation: The physical layer (Layer 1) deals with the transmission of raw bits over a physical medium, defining electrical, mechanical, and timing specifications.

text: What is a network protocol stack?
options: A layered set of protocols working together
A single protocol handling all network tasks
A hardware component inside every router
A physical rack holding network equipment
explanation: A protocol stack is a layered set of protocols that work together to provide network communication, with each layer handling specific functions.

text: What is the primary difference between a Layer 2 switch and a Layer 3 switch?
options: Layer 2 switches support wireless clients while Layer 3 cannot
Layer 3 switches can perform routing based on IP addresses
Layer 2 switches are faster but less reliable than Layer 3 switches
Layer 3 switches only operate in star topology networks
explanation: A Layer 3 switch combines switching and routing functionality, making forwarding decisions based on IP addresses in addition to MAC addresses used by Layer 2 switches.

text: What is the purpose of the encapsulation process in the OSI model?
options: To remove headers as data moves down the layers
To compress data at each layer for faster transmission
To encrypt data at every layer for maximum security
To add protocol information as data descends through layers
explanation: Encapsulation adds protocol-specific header (and sometimes trailer) information at each layer as data moves down the OSI model, preparing it for transmission.

text: What is the function of the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)?
options: It assigns dynamic IP addresses to new network devices
It resolves port numbers to specific running applications
It maps IP addresses to their corresponding MAC addresses
It maps domain names to their corresponding IP addresses
explanation: ARP resolves (maps) a known IP address to the corresponding MAC address on a local network, enabling data link layer communication.

text: How does a switch learn which MAC addresses are on which ports?
options: By examining the source MAC address of incoming frames
By reading configuration files loaded at startup
By querying a central MAC address database server
By broadcasting ARP requests to all connected devices
explanation: A switch builds its MAC address table by examining the source MAC address of incoming frames and associating it with the port on which the frame arrived.

text: What is the difference between unicast, multicast, and broadcast transmissions?
options: Broadcast sends to one, unicast to a group, multicast to all
Multicast sends to one, broadcast to a group, unicast to all
Unicast sends to all, broadcast to a group, multicast to one
Unicast sends to one, multicast to a group, broadcast to all
explanation: Unicast sends data to a single destination, multicast sends to a specific group of destinations, and broadcast sends to all devices on the network.

text: What does the data link layer PDU (Protocol Data Unit) consist of?
options: Frames with MAC headers and trailer fields
Packets with IP headers and payload data
Bits transmitted as raw signals on the wire
Segments with port numbers and sequence info
explanation: The data link layer PDU is called a frame, which consists of a header (containing MAC addresses), the payload data, and a trailer (containing error-checking information).

text: What is store-and-forward switching in a network switch?
options: The switch only forwards broadcast frames to all ports
The switch receives the entire frame before forwarding it
The switch forwards frames before fully receiving them
The switch stores frames indefinitely until requested
explanation: In store-and-forward switching, the switch receives the entire frame, checks it for errors using the FCS field, and then forwards it to the appropriate port.

text: What is the purpose of RARP (Reverse Address Resolution Protocol)?
options: It translates private IP addresses to public ones
It resolves hostnames into IP address records
It converts MAC addresses back to IP addresses
It maps port numbers to application layer services
explanation: RARP allows a device that knows its MAC address to discover its IP address, effectively performing the reverse of ARP.

text: In a network with multiple paths, what advantage does load balancing provide?
options: It assigns static IP addresses to all network devices
It blocks redundant paths to simplify routing tables
It distributes traffic across paths to optimize utilization
It encrypts all traffic across different network paths
explanation: Load balancing distributes network traffic across multiple paths or servers to optimize resource utilization, minimize response time, and avoid overloading any single resource.

text: What is the role of the session layer in the OSI model?
options: It converts data formats between different system types
It handles physical transmission of bits across the medium
It performs routing and logical addressing for packets
It manages dialog control and synchronization between hosts
explanation: The session layer (Layer 5) manages dialog control, synchronization, and session establishment/maintenance/termination between communicating applications.

text: What is the primary purpose of the Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (RSTP) over STP?
options: RSTP enables routing capabilities within Layer 2 switch devices
RSTP provides significantly faster convergence after topology changes
RSTP supports more VLANs per switch than standard STP protocol
RSTP eliminates the need for root bridge election in networks
explanation: RSTP (IEEE 802.1w) significantly reduces convergence time from STP's 30-50 seconds to just a few seconds by introducing new port roles and states.

text: In the context of SDN, what is the function of the southbound API?
options: It enables communication between the controller and network devices
It provides network analytics to management applications
It connects external applications to the SDN controller layer
It manages user authentication for the SDN control plane
explanation: The southbound API (such as OpenFlow) enables communication between the SDN controller and the network infrastructure devices (switches, routers) to program their forwarding behavior.

text: What is the purpose of ECMP (Equal-Cost Multi-Path) in network design?
options: To encrypt multiple paths for secure data delivery
To create backup paths that activate only during failure
To distribute traffic across multiple equal-cost routes
To select the single lowest-cost path and block others
explanation: ECMP allows routers to distribute traffic across multiple paths that have the same routing metric, improving throughput and providing redundancy.

text: What is a fabric network architecture in data center design?
options: A legacy three-tier design using core, distribution, and access layers
A bus topology where all servers share a single backbone cable
A ring-based topology connecting servers in a circular pattern
A flat, scalable topology using spine-and-leaf switch arrangement
explanation: A fabric network architecture uses a spine-and-leaf design where every leaf switch connects to every spine switch, providing consistent low latency and high bandwidth in data centers.

text: What is control plane policing (CoPP) used for in network infrastructure?
options: To protect the router's control plane from excessive traffic
To prioritize user data traffic over management traffic
To balance routing protocol traffic across multiple interfaces
To encrypt all control plane communications between routers
explanation: CoPP protects the router's control plane (CPU) from being overwhelmed by excessive traffic such as routing protocol floods, DoS attacks, or reconnaissance scans.

text: What is the difference between in-band and out-of-band network management?
options: In-band management works only for wired networks and out-of-band only for wireless
In-band uses production network paths while out-of-band uses dedicated management channels
Out-of-band uses production network paths while in-band uses dedicated management channels
Out-of-band management requires specialized hardware while in-band uses standard NICs
explanation: In-band management uses the same network paths as production traffic, while out-of-band uses separate dedicated management channels, providing access even when the production network is down.

text: What is the purpose of BFD (Bidirectional Forwarding Detection) in networks?
options: To authenticate routing protocol messages between peer routers
To encrypt bidirectional traffic between two network devices
To balance network traffic bidirectionally across redundant paths
To provide rapid failure detection between adjacent forwarding engines
explanation: BFD is a lightweight protocol that provides rapid failure detection between two adjacent forwarding engines, enabling faster convergence than relying on routing protocol timers alone.

text: In MPLS, what is the purpose of the label distribution protocol (LDP)?
options: It converts IP headers into MPLS labels at network edges
It establishes label-switched paths by distributing label bindings
It encrypts MPLS labels to prevent label spoofing attacks
It monitors label utilization and optimizes path selection
explanation: LDP is used in MPLS networks to distribute label binding information between routers, establishing Label Switched Paths (LSPs) for efficient packet forwarding.

text: What is the concept of network telemetry and how does it differ from SNMP polling?
options: Telemetry pushes data in real-time while SNMP uses periodic polling
Telemetry is limited to local networks while SNMP works globally
Telemetry requires manual data collection while SNMP is automated
Telemetry only monitors hardware while SNMP monitors software too
explanation: Network telemetry uses a push-based model where devices stream operational data in real-time, unlike SNMP which relies on periodic polling that can miss transient events.

text: What is the role of a route reflector in large-scale BGP deployments?
options: It converts eBGP routes into OSPF routes for internal distribution
It encrypts BGP update messages between internal BGP speakers
It reduces the need for full mesh iBGP peering by reflecting routes
It filters malicious BGP routes from external autonomous systems
explanation: A route reflector eliminates the need for full-mesh iBGP by allowing certain routers to reflect (re-advertise) learned routes to other iBGP peers, improving scalability.

text: What is the purpose of flow control at the data link layer?
options: To encrypt data before physical transmission
To prevent the sender from overwhelming the receiver
To route frames between different networks
To assign IP addresses to connected devices
explanation: Flow control at the data link layer prevents a fast sender from overwhelming a slower receiver by regulating the rate of data transmission.

text: What is the stop-and-wait protocol in data link communication?
options: The sender transmits all frames at once before waiting
The receiver stops all incoming traffic when busy
The sender waits for a timer before each transmission
The sender sends one frame and waits for acknowledgment
explanation: In the stop-and-wait protocol, the sender transmits one frame and then waits for an acknowledgment from the receiver before sending the next frame.

text: What does ARQ stand for in data link layer protocols?
options: Automatic Repeat reQuest for error recovery
Asynchronous Relay Queue for data buffering
Address Resolution Queue for frame delivery
Adaptive Rate Quantization for bandwidth use
explanation: ARQ stands for Automatic Repeat reQuest, a group of error-control protocols where the receiver requests retransmission of frames that are received with errors.

text: What is the broadcast MAC address used to reach all devices on a network?
options: 00:00:00:00:00:00 representing zero address
01:00:00:00:00:00 representing multicast
FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF representing all ones
FE:FE:FE:FE:FE:FE representing link local
explanation: The broadcast MAC address is FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF (all bits set to 1), which is used to send a frame to all devices on the local network segment.

text: What type of error occurs when a single bit in a frame is changed during transmission?
options: A burst error affecting multiple bits
A framing error at the boundary mark
A single-bit error in the data frame
A sequence error in the frame order
explanation: A single-bit error occurs when only one bit of the transmitted frame is altered (flipped from 0 to 1 or vice versa) during transmission.

text: What is the purpose of the destination address field in an Ethernet frame?
options: To identify the intended recipient device
To indicate the protocol type being used
To contain error-checking information data
To specify the sender's network location
explanation: The destination address field in an Ethernet frame contains the MAC address of the intended recipient device so that the frame is delivered to the correct destination.

text: What is the role of acknowledgment frames in reliable data delivery?
options: They establish the initial connection setup
They confirm successful receipt of data frames
They carry user data from sender to receiver
They request retransmission of all sent frames
explanation: Acknowledgment (ACK) frames are sent by the receiver to confirm that data frames were received successfully, enabling the sender to know transmission was completed.

text: What is a collision in the context of Ethernet networks?
options: When a cable is physically disconnected from a port
When a device sends data to an unknown MAC address
When a frame exceeds the maximum allowed frame size
When two devices transmit data simultaneously on shared media
explanation: A collision occurs when two or more devices attempt to transmit data simultaneously on a shared communication medium, causing the signals to interfere with each other.

text: What is the function of the type/length field in an Ethernet frame?
options: It contains the frame check sequence value
It indicates the protocol or payload length
It stores the MAC address of the next hop
It specifies the transmission speed to use
explanation: The type/length field in an Ethernet frame indicates either the upper-layer protocol (like IPv4 or IPv6) or the length of the payload data depending on its value.

text: What does the term 'full-duplex Ethernet' mean?
options: Data can only flow in one direction at a time
Data is transmitted at double the normal speed
Data can flow in both directions simultaneously
Data is duplicated across two separate cables
explanation: Full-duplex Ethernet allows simultaneous transmission and reception of data, effectively doubling the throughput and eliminating collisions.

text: In the Go-Back-N protocol, what does the sender do when a timer expires for an unacknowledged frame?
options: It waits for the receiver to send a negative acknowledgment
It resends only the specific frame that timed out
It closes the connection and initiates a new session
It resends the timed-out frame and all subsequent sent frames
explanation: In Go-Back-N, when a timer expires, the sender retransmits the frame that timed out and all subsequent frames that were sent after it, even if some were received correctly.

text: What is the difference between error detection and error correction?
options: Detection finds errors and requests retransmission while correction fixes them directly
Detection finds and fixes errors while correction only finds them
Detection uses parity bits while correction uses checksum only
Detection works only for burst errors while correction handles single-bit errors
explanation: Error detection identifies that an error has occurred and typically requests retransmission, while error correction (like Hamming code) can identify and fix errors without retransmission.

text: What is the purpose of the interframe gap in Ethernet transmission?
options: To synchronize clocks of all devices on the network
To encrypt the gap between successive data frames
To carry management data between consecutive frames
To provide minimum idle time between frame transmissions
explanation: The interframe gap (IFG) provides a minimum idle period (96 bit times) between frame transmissions, allowing receiving devices to process the previous frame before the next arrives.

text: How does a two-dimensional parity check improve over simple parity?
options: It only works with even numbers of data bytes in each block
It can detect and correct single-bit errors in the data block
It uses half the number of check bits compared to simple parity
It eliminates the need for any retransmission of faulty frames
explanation: Two-dimensional parity arranges data in rows and columns with parity bits for each, enabling it to detect and correct single-bit errors by identifying the row and column of the error.

text: What is the piggybacking technique in data link layer protocols?
options: Attaching acknowledgments to outgoing data frames to save bandwidth
Compressing frame headers to reduce per-frame transmission overhead
Duplicating frames across two links for redundancy and reliability
Sending multiple data frames inside a single larger container frame
explanation: Piggybacking involves attaching acknowledgment information to outgoing data frames rather than sending separate ACK frames, improving bandwidth efficiency.

text: In CSMA/CD, what is the minimum frame size related to?
options: The maximum number of devices on the network segment
The total available bandwidth on the shared cable medium
The round-trip propagation delay of the collision domain
The processor speed of the transmitting network device
explanation: The minimum frame size in CSMA/CD is determined by the round-trip propagation delay of the collision domain, ensuring that a sender is still transmitting when it detects a collision.

text: What is the spanning tree port state 'blocking' used for?
options: The port is disabled and cannot receive any traffic
The port is actively forwarding user data frames
The port does not forward frames to prevent network loops
The port is learning MAC addresses from incoming frames
explanation: In the blocking state, a spanning tree port does not forward user data frames but still listens for BPDUs to prevent network loops while maintaining awareness of topology changes.

text: What is the purpose of the jam signal in CSMA/CD?
options: To request priority access to the shared transmission medium
To notify all stations that a collision has been detected
To test the cable for faults before data is transmitted
To synchronize the clocks of all stations on the network
explanation: When a collision is detected in CSMA/CD, the transmitting station sends a jam signal to ensure all other stations on the network are aware that a collision occurred.

text: What is the role of the Logical Link Control (LLC) sublayer in frame handling?
options: It provides an interface between the network layer and MAC sublayer
It manages collision detection on shared Ethernet segments
It handles physical signal encoding on the transmission medium
It assigns physical addresses to each networked device
explanation: The LLC sublayer provides an interface between the network layer and the MAC sublayer, handling flow control, error notification, and identifying the upper-layer protocol.

text: What is a transparent bridge and how does it operate?
options: It requires manual configuration of all forwarding table entries
It forwards frames based on IP addresses and routing tables
It operates only between networks using different data link protocols
It learns and forwards frames based on MAC addresses invisibly to hosts
explanation: A transparent bridge automatically learns MAC addresses and makes forwarding decisions without any configuration needed by end hosts, operating invisibly to the network devices.

text: What is the throughput efficiency of the pure ALOHA protocol expressed as a fraction?
options: Approximately 18.4% maximum channel utilization
Approximately 36.8% maximum channel utilization
Approximately 50.0% maximum channel utilization
Approximately 25.0% maximum channel utilization
explanation: Pure ALOHA achieves a maximum throughput of 1/(2e), approximately 18.4%, because any frame sent within twice the frame transmission time can cause a collision.

text: In the CRC error detection scheme, if the generator polynomial is x^4 + x + 1, how many check bits are appended?
options: 5 check bits are appended to the data
3 check bits are appended to the data
6 check bits are appended to the data
4 check bits are appended to the data
explanation: The number of check bits in CRC equals the degree of the generator polynomial. For x^4 + x + 1, the degree is 4, so 4 check bits (remainder bits) are appended.

text: What is the maximum throughput efficiency of slotted ALOHA compared to pure ALOHA?
options: Slotted ALOHA triples throughput to about 55.2% by using time slots
Slotted ALOHA quadruples throughput to about 73.6% by time slots
Slotted ALOHA doubles throughput to about 36.8% by using time slots
Slotted ALOHA achieves the same throughput as pure ALOHA protocol
explanation: Slotted ALOHA achieves maximum throughput of 1/e (about 36.8%), exactly double that of pure ALOHA (1/2e or 18.4%), by requiring transmissions to begin at slot boundaries.

text: In a Hamming code with 11-bit data words, how many redundancy bits are required?
options: 3 redundancy bits for 11 data bits
4 redundancy bits for 11 data bits
5 redundancy bits for 11 data bits
6 redundancy bits for 11 data bits
explanation: For Hamming code, the formula 2^r >= d + r + 1 must be satisfied. With d=11, r=4 gives 2^4=16 >= 11+4+1=16, so 4 redundancy bits are needed.

text: What is the difference between 1-persistent and p-persistent CSMA?
options: p-persistent transmits immediately when idle while 1-persistent transmits with probability p
p-persistent is used only in wireless networks while 1-persistent is for wired only
1-persistent waits a random time while p-persistent waits a fixed time period
1-persistent transmits immediately when idle while p-persistent transmits with probability p
explanation: In 1-persistent CSMA, a station transmits immediately when it finds the channel idle. In p-persistent CSMA, it transmits with probability p when the channel is idle, waiting with probability 1-p.

text: What is the purpose of the BPDU (Bridge Protocol Data Unit) in spanning tree operation?
options: It exchanges topology information between bridges for loop prevention
It carries user data frames between bridges in the network
It encrypts forwarding table entries for secure bridge operation
It assigns IP addresses to bridge ports during initialization
explanation: BPDUs are special frames exchanged between bridges to share information about bridge IDs, port costs, and root bridge identity, enabling the Spanning Tree Protocol to prevent loops.

text: In Link Aggregation (IEEE 802.3ad), what is the primary benefit of combining multiple physical links?
options: It enables different speed links to operate at the same data rate
It increases bandwidth and provides link redundancy as one logical link
It eliminates the need for spanning tree on aggregated link ports
It reduces the MAC address table size on connected switches
explanation: Link Aggregation (LAG/LACP) combines multiple physical links into one logical link, increasing aggregate bandwidth and providing link-level redundancy if one physical link fails.

text: What is the channel capture effect in CSMA/CD networks?
options: When the broadcast domain captures all unicast frames on the segment
When a switch port captures and mirrors all traffic for monitoring
When one station dominates the channel because its backoff counter resets to zero after success
When a collision causes all stations to simultaneously attempt retransmission
explanation: The channel capture effect occurs when a successful station resets its backoff counter to zero, giving it a statistical advantage over stations with higher backoff values from prior collisions.

text: What is the purpose of the 802.1Q native VLAN concept?
options: It handles untagged frames on a trunk link by assigning them to a default VLAN
It reserves bandwidth for management traffic between switches
It encrypts inter-VLAN routing traffic on Layer 3 switches
It assigns all unknown unicast traffic to a quarantine VLAN
explanation: The native VLAN in 802.1Q is the VLAN assigned to untagged frames on a trunk link. Frames belonging to the native VLAN are sent without a VLAN tag.

text: How does the token bucket algorithm differ from the leaky bucket algorithm for traffic shaping?
options: Token bucket discards excess tokens while leaky bucket stores them indefinitely
Leaky bucket allows bursts up to a limit while token bucket enforces a constant rate
Leaky bucket uses tokens for rate control while token bucket uses timers only
Token bucket allows bursts up to a limit while leaky bucket enforces a constant rate
explanation: The token bucket algorithm allows traffic bursts up to a maximum bucket size while maintaining an average rate, whereas the leaky bucket enforces a strict constant output rate regardless of input burstiness.

text: What is the purpose of an IP address in networking?
options: To specify the application protocol being used
To logically identify a device on a network
To determine the cable type connecting devices
To identify the physical hardware of a device
explanation: An IP address provides a logical identifier for a device on a network, enabling routing of data packets to the correct destination across interconnected networks.

text: What is the difference between a public and a private IP address?
options: Public addresses are assigned by DHCP while private addresses are manually configured
Private addresses use IPv6 while public addresses always use IPv4 format
Private addresses are used within local networks while public addresses are on the internet
Public addresses are used on local networks while private addresses are on the internet
explanation: Private IP addresses are used within local networks (not routable on the internet), while public IP addresses are globally unique and routable on the internet.

text: What is the default subnet mask for a Class A IPv4 address?
options: 255.255.255.0 with 24 network bits
255.255.255.128 with 25 network bits
255.0.0.0 with 8 network bits only
255.255.0.0 with 16 network bits
explanation: Class A addresses use a default subnet mask of 255.0.0.0, meaning the first 8 bits identify the network and the remaining 24 bits identify hosts.

text: What is a routing table used for in a router?
options: To determine the best path for forwarding packets
To filter web content based on user preferences
To encrypt all outgoing traffic at the gateway
To store the physical locations of all devices
explanation: A routing table contains entries that map destination network addresses to next-hop addresses and output interfaces, helping the router determine the best path for forwarding packets.

text: Which class of IPv4 addresses has a first octet range of 128 to 191?
options: Class D addresses for multicast groups
Class C addresses for small networks
Class A addresses for large networks
Class B addresses for medium networks
explanation: Class B IPv4 addresses have a first octet in the range 128-191, designed for medium-sized organizations with the default subnet mask 255.255.0.0.

text: What is static routing in a network?
options: Routes change dynamically based on current traffic load
Routes are manually configured by a network administrator
Routes are computed using the shortest path algorithm
Routes are automatically learned from neighboring routers
explanation: Static routing involves manually configuring routes in the routing table by a network administrator, as opposed to dynamic routing where routes are learned automatically.

text: What does the traceroute (tracert) command show?
options: The MAC addresses of all devices on a local network
The list of open ports on a destination host
The bandwidth available on each network interface
The path packets take through routers to a destination
explanation: Traceroute shows the path (sequence of routers) that packets take from the source to the destination, along with the round-trip time to each hop.

text: What is an IPv4 header's minimum size in bytes?
options: 20 bytes without any option fields
32 bytes without any option fields
10 bytes without any option fields
40 bytes without any option fields
explanation: The minimum IPv4 header size is 20 bytes (5 words of 32 bits each), with no optional fields included.

text: What type of routing protocol learns routes automatically from other routers?
options: Direct routing for locally connected subnets
Default routing using a single gateway path
Dynamic routing using protocol exchanges
Static routing configured manually by admin
explanation: Dynamic routing protocols (like RIP, OSPF, BGP) automatically learn and update routes by exchanging routing information with neighboring routers.

text: What is the purpose of the network layer's fragmentation mechanism?
options: To combine small packets into larger ones for efficiency
To split packets that exceed a link's maximum transfer unit
To compress large packets for faster transmission speed
To encrypt packet contents for secure delivery to hosts
explanation: Fragmentation at the network layer divides packets that are larger than the Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) of a link into smaller fragments that can be transmitted.

text: What is the administrative distance used for in routing?
options: It measures the physical distance between two routers in meters
It ranks the trustworthiness of routing information sources
It calculates the total bandwidth available on a route path
It determines the maximum number of hops allowed on a route
explanation: Administrative distance is a value assigned to routing information sources to rank their trustworthiness. Lower values indicate more trusted sources, helping routers choose between routes learned from different protocols.

text: In OSPF, what is a Link State Advertisement (LSA)?
options: A message containing information about a router's links and their states
A request sent to the designated router for database synchronization
A packet used to discover neighboring OSPF routers on a link
An acknowledgment confirming receipt of routing table updates
explanation: An LSA is a message that OSPF routers use to share information about their directly connected links and their states, which is used to build the link-state database.

text: What is the purpose of the Don't Fragment (DF) flag in the IPv4 header?
options: It marks the packet as the final fragment in a sequence
It indicates the packet has already been fragmented once
It instructs routers not to fragment the packet along the path
It requests priority handling to avoid fragmentation delays
explanation: The DF flag tells routers along the path not to fragment the packet. If a router cannot forward the packet without fragmenting it, the packet is dropped and an ICMP error is sent.

text: How does proxy ARP work in a network?
options: A switch duplicates ARP requests and sends them to all VLANs
A host caches ARP entries for all devices on the entire internet
A firewall blocks ARP requests from unauthorized network segments
A router answers ARP requests on behalf of hosts on another network
explanation: Proxy ARP allows a router to respond to ARP requests on behalf of devices on a different network segment, making those remote devices appear to be on the local network.

text: What is the difference between RIPv1 and RIPv2?
options: RIPv1 supports VLSM and authentication while RIPv2 does not
RIPv2 supports VLSM and multicast updates while RIPv1 does not
RIPv2 has unlimited hop count while RIPv1 is limited to 15 hops
RIPv1 uses link-state algorithm while RIPv2 uses distance-vector
explanation: RIPv2 improves over RIPv1 by supporting Variable Length Subnet Masking (VLSM), authentication, and sending updates via multicast (224.0.0.9) instead of broadcast.

text: What is the purpose of the OSPF hello protocol?
options: To discover neighbors and maintain adjacency relationships
To redistribute routes from other routing protocols into OSPF
To calculate the shortest path using Dijkstra algorithm
To exchange full routing tables between all OSPF routers
explanation: The OSPF hello protocol discovers neighboring OSPF routers, establishes and maintains adjacencies, and detects neighbor failures through periodic hello packet exchanges.

text: What is the purpose of the Identification field in the IPv4 header?
options: It determines the priority level of the packet for QoS processing
It specifies the version of the IP protocol being used
It stores the checksum value for header integrity verification
It uniquely identifies fragments belonging to the same original packet
explanation: The Identification field contains a unique value assigned by the sender, used to identify all fragments that belong to the same original IP packet for reassembly at the destination.

text: What is the longest prefix match rule in IP routing?
options: The route with the most hops to the destination is preferred
The route learned from the oldest routing protocol is chosen
The route with the most specific (longest) subnet mask is selected
The route with the lowest administrative distance is preferred
explanation: The longest prefix match rule states that when multiple routing table entries match a destination, the entry with the longest (most specific) subnet mask is selected for forwarding.

text: What is the multicast address range in IPv4?
explanation: IPv4 multicast addresses use the Class D range from 224.0.0.0 to 239.255.255.255, allowing a single packet to be delivered to a group of interested receivers.

text: What is the purpose of ICMP redirect messages?
options: To request a DNS resolver to redirect queries elsewhere
To redirect all network traffic through a backup gateway
To inform a host of a better route for a specific destination
To notify the sender that a packet has been fragmented
explanation: ICMP redirect messages are sent by a router to inform a host on the same network that there is a better (more direct) route available for reaching a specific destination.

text: Given the address 10.45.128.0/17, how many host addresses are available in this subnet?
options: 32,766 usable host addresses available
131,070 usable host addresses available
65,534 usable host addresses available
16,382 usable host addresses available
explanation: A /17 subnet has 32 - 17 = 15 host bits, giving 2^15 - 2 = 32,766 usable host addresses (excluding network and broadcast addresses).

text: In OSPF, what is the purpose of stub areas?
options: They serve as the backbone area connecting all other OSPF areas
They handle inter-area routing between non-backbone OSPF areas
They contain all external routes and redistribute them to other areas
They block external route advertisements to reduce routing table size
explanation: OSPF stub areas do not accept external route advertisements (Type 5 LSAs), replacing them with a default route to reduce routing table size and link-state database overhead.

text: What is the purpose of the BGP LOCAL_PREF attribute?
options: It tracks the sequence of autonomous systems a route has traversed
It specifies the physical distance to the destination network prefix
It indicates the preference for exit paths within an autonomous system
It assigns a community tag for route filtering between providers
explanation: LOCAL_PREF is shared among iBGP peers within an AS to indicate the preferred exit path for outbound traffic. Higher LOCAL_PREF values are preferred.

text: What is the difference between NAT and PAT (Port Address Translation)?
options: PAT maps each private IP to a unique public IP while NAT uses port-based multiplexing
PAT supports only TCP traffic while NAT supports both TCP and UDP protocols
NAT operates at the data link layer while PAT operates at the network layer only
NAT maps each private IP to a unique public IP while PAT maps many to one using ports
explanation: NAT maps private IP addresses to public IP addresses. PAT (also called NAT overload) maps multiple private IP addresses to a single public IP address by using different port numbers.

text: What is the poison reverse technique in distance-vector routing?
options: Setting the metric to infinity for routes learned from a neighbor back to that neighbor
Sending routing updates with encrypted metrics to prevent tampering by attackers
Removing failed routes immediately from the routing table without waiting for timers
Blocking all routing updates from a specific neighbor for a fixed timeout period
explanation: Poison reverse improves upon split horizon by explicitly advertising routes learned from a neighbor back to that neighbor with an infinite metric, clearly indicating the route is unreachable via that path.

text: In IPv6, what is a link-local address and what prefix does it use?
options: A multicast group address using the ff00::/8 address prefix
An address valid only on a single link using the fe80::/10 prefix
A loopback address using the ::1/128 single host address
A globally routable address using the 2001::/16 address prefix
explanation: A link-local address in IPv6 uses the prefix fe80::/10 and is valid only on the local link. It is automatically configured on every IPv6-enabled interface and used for neighbor discovery.

text: What is the purpose of the OSPF virtual link?
options: To connect non-backbone areas that are not physically adjacent to Area 0
To redistribute external routes from BGP into the OSPF routing domain
To load balance traffic between two equal-cost paths in the backbone
To provide encrypted tunnels between OSPF routers across the internet
explanation: An OSPF virtual link provides logical connectivity to Area 0 (backbone) for areas that are not physically connected to it, maintaining the requirement that all areas connect to the backbone.

text: What is Gratuitous ARP and what is its primary use case?
options: An ARP message that assigns new IP addresses to devices joining a network
An ARP reply sent in response to a multicast discovery query from a router
An ARP request where the sender asks for its own IP to detect address conflicts
An ARP update used to flush old MAC entries from neighboring device tables
explanation: Gratuitous ARP is an ARP request where the sender queries its own IP address. It is primarily used to detect IP address conflicts and to update ARP caches on the local network.

text: In BGP route selection, what is the MED (Multi-Exit Discriminator) attribute used for?
options: To indicate the maximum number of paths available for equal-cost load balancing
To determine the originating router's preference for internal path selection
To specify the exit point for traffic leaving the local autonomous system
To suggest preferred entry points into an AS to neighboring autonomous systems
explanation: MED is an optional non-transitive BGP attribute used to suggest to external neighbors which entry point into an AS is preferred when multiple entry points exist. Lower MED is preferred.

text: What is the purpose of the Router Solicitation and Router Advertisement messages in IPv6?
options: They establish IPSec security associations between IPv6 endpoints
They enable hosts to discover routers and obtain network configuration
They provide DNS resolution services for IPv6 host name lookups
They are used for multicast group membership management on a link
explanation: Router Solicitation and Router Advertisement messages are part of IPv6 Neighbor Discovery Protocol, enabling hosts to discover routers on the link and obtain prefix and configuration information.

text: What is the primary difference between TCP and UDP?
options: TCP provides reliable delivery while UDP is connectionless
TCP works only on wired networks while UDP works wirelessly
TCP is faster while UDP provides guaranteed delivery
UDP uses larger packets while TCP uses smaller packets
explanation: TCP provides reliable, connection-oriented delivery with error checking and retransmission, while UDP is a connectionless protocol that provides fast but unreliable delivery.

text: What is a well-known port number?
options: A port used exclusively by operating system processes
A dynamically assigned port used for temporary connections
A port in the range 0-1023 reserved for standard services
Any port above 49151 used by client applications
explanation: Well-known ports range from 0 to 1023 and are reserved for standard services and protocols, such as HTTP (80), FTP (21), and SSH (22).

text: Which port number does DNS typically use?
options: Port 23 for domain name queries
Port 53 for domain name queries
Port 67 for domain name queries
Port 110 for domain name queries
explanation: DNS uses port 53 for both TCP and UDP communications. UDP is typically used for standard queries while TCP is used for zone transfers and large responses.

text: What is multiplexing at the transport layer?
options: Converting data formats between different application types
Splitting a single application across multiple networks
Encrypting traffic from a single application for security
Combining data from multiple applications for transmission
explanation: Transport layer multiplexing combines data from multiple applications (using port numbers) into a single stream for transmission over the network.

text: What flag is used in the TCP handshake to initiate a connection?
options: The ACK flag for acknowledgment setup
The SYN flag for synchronization request
The FIN flag for connection finalization
The RST flag for connection reset action
explanation: The SYN (synchronize) flag is used to initiate a TCP connection. The client sends a SYN packet to the server as the first step of the three-way handshake.

text: What application layer service typically uses TCP port 25?
options: Email sending using SMTP protocol
Secure shell remote login to servers
Web browsing using HTTP protocol
File transfer using FTP data channel
explanation: Port 25 is used by SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) for sending email between mail servers.

text: What is the purpose of sequence numbers in TCP?
options: To measure the round-trip time for each packet
To encrypt the data payload of each segment
To identify the source application on the host
To track the order of transmitted data bytes
explanation: TCP sequence numbers track the order of data bytes, enabling the receiver to reassemble segments in the correct order and detect missing data.

text: Why is UDP preferred for live video streaming applications?
options: UDP guarantees all video frames arrive in order
UDP provides better encryption for video content
UDP has lower overhead and tolerates some data loss
UDP supports larger maximum packet sizes than TCP
explanation: UDP is preferred for live streaming because its low overhead reduces latency, and occasional packet loss is acceptable in real-time media where retransmission would cause delays.

text: What is demultiplexing at the transport layer?
options: Converting between different transport protocols dynamically
Combining data from many applications into one stream
Delivering received data to the correct application via port numbers
Splitting data across multiple network paths for speed
explanation: Demultiplexing is the process of delivering incoming transport-layer segments to the correct application by examining the destination port number in the segment header.

text: What is an ephemeral port in networking?
options: A port used exclusively for encrypted communication
A permanently assigned port for a specific service
A reserved port used only by system administrators
A temporary port assigned to a client for a session
explanation: An ephemeral (temporary) port is dynamically assigned to a client application for the duration of a communication session, typically in the range 49152-65535.

text: How does TCP's sliding window mechanism improve throughput?
options: It compresses data within each segment to reduce packet size
It allows multiple segments to be sent before requiring acknowledgment
It chooses the fastest network path for each individual segment
It eliminates the need for sequence numbers in the TCP header
explanation: TCP's sliding window allows the sender to transmit multiple segments without waiting for individual acknowledgments, significantly improving throughput compared to stop-and-wait.

text: What is the purpose of the TCP Maximum Segment Size (MSS) option?
options: It specifies the largest data payload a segment can carry
It sets the timeout value for retransmission of segments
It limits the total number of segments in a connection
It determines the maximum window size for flow control
explanation: MSS specifies the maximum amount of data (in bytes) that a TCP endpoint is willing to receive in a single segment, typically negotiated during the three-way handshake.

text: What happens during the TCP connection termination process?
options: Both sides exchange FIN and ACK segments in a four-step process
A single FIN segment from either side terminates the connection
The server unilaterally closes the connection without client input
One side sends a RST and the connection immediately closes
explanation: TCP uses a four-step graceful termination: one side sends FIN, the other ACKs it, then sends its own FIN, which is ACKed by the first side, ensuring both sides close properly.

text: What is the TCP retransmission timeout (RTO) based on?
options: The number of segments currently in the send buffer queue
Estimated round-trip time and its variation for the connection
A fixed interval of exactly 3 seconds for all connections
The bandwidth of the network interface used for transmission
explanation: TCP's RTO is dynamically calculated based on the estimated round-trip time (RTT) and its variance using algorithms like Jacobson's, adapting to current network conditions.

text: What is the difference between TCP's cumulative and selective acknowledgment?
options: Cumulative ACKs work only with UDP while selective ACK works only with TCP
Selective ACK confirms all data up to a point while cumulative identifies specific ranges
Selective ACK requires more bandwidth but cumulative ACK causes more retransmissions
Cumulative ACKs confirm all data up to a point while SACK identifies specific received ranges
explanation: Cumulative ACK acknowledges all data received up to a byte number, while SACK (Selective ACK) additionally identifies non-contiguous blocks of received data, reducing unnecessary retransmissions.

text: What is the purpose of the TCP congestion window (cwnd)?
options: It limits the total number of connections a server can accept
It determines the priority of segments during network congestion
It controls how much data the sender can transmit before receiving ACKs
It specifies the maximum receive buffer size at the destination
explanation: The congestion window (cwnd) limits the amount of unacknowledged data the sender can have in the network, dynamically adjusting based on perceived network congestion.

text: What is delayed acknowledgment in TCP?
options: The sender delays retransmission until multiple timeouts have occurred
The sender delays sending data until it accumulates a full-size segment
The receiver discards ACKs for duplicate segments to save bandwidth
The receiver waits briefly before sending an ACK hoping to piggyback it with data
explanation: Delayed ACK is a TCP optimization where the receiver waits a short time (up to 500ms) before sending an ACK, hoping to piggyback it with outgoing data to reduce the number of pure ACK packets.

text: How does UDP handle data that arrives out of order?
options: UDP buffers packets until all segments arrive then delivers them
UDP discards out-of-order packets and requests retransmission
UDP delivers data to the application in whatever order it arrives
UDP reorders segments using sequence numbers before delivery
explanation: UDP does not provide ordering guarantees. It delivers data to the application in the order it arrives, leaving any ordering requirements to the application layer.

text: What does the TCP window scale option allow?
options: It enables window sizes larger than the 16-bit field allows
It dynamically adjusts the MSS during data transfer
It reduces the header size for low-bandwidth connections
It compresses window update notifications between peers
explanation: The TCP window scale option multiplies the 16-bit window size field by a power of 2 (up to 2^14), enabling window sizes up to 1 GB for high-bandwidth, high-latency connections.

text: What is the purpose of the TCP keepalive mechanism?
options: To periodically refresh the routing path for the TCP connection
To detect if the other end of an idle connection is still reachable
To prevent the congestion window from shrinking during idle time
To maintain data throughput at a constant rate during transfers
explanation: TCP keepalive sends periodic probe segments on idle connections to verify that the other endpoint is still alive and reachable, detecting broken connections.

text: In TCP congestion control, what is the additive increase multiplicative decrease (AIMD) principle?
options: Window stays constant during data transfer and doubles when congestion is cleared
Window increases exponentially during slow start and resets to one on any timeout
Window doubles on each ACK and halves on congestion detection for rapid adaptation
Window increases by one MSS per RTT and halves on congestion for stable convergence
explanation: AIMD increases the congestion window by one MSS per RTT (additive increase) during congestion avoidance and halves it upon detecting congestion (multiplicative decrease), proven to converge to fair bandwidth allocation.

text: What is the TCP CUBIC algorithm and when is it commonly used?
options: A retransmission algorithm that groups lost segments into cubic-sized recovery blocks
A congestion control algorithm using a cubic function for window growth in high-BDP networks
A scheduling algorithm that assigns cubic priority levels to competing TCP flows
A flow control algorithm that triples the window size after each successful round trip
explanation: TCP CUBIC uses a cubic mathematical function for window growth, making it less aggressive near the previous congestion point and more aggressive further away. It is the default in Linux kernels for high bandwidth-delay product networks.

text: What is the TCP fast open (TFO) mechanism designed to reduce?
options: The CPU cost of computing TCP checksums on high-throughput connections
The overhead of maintaining idle TCP connection state on servers
The latency of TCP connection establishment by sending data in the SYN
The bandwidth consumed by TCP keepalive probes on long-lived connections
explanation: TCP Fast Open reduces connection establishment latency by allowing data to be sent in the initial SYN packet using a cryptographic cookie, eliminating one RTT delay for repeat connections.

text: What is the fundamental problem that QUIC's connection migration solves?
options: The inability to maintain a session when a client's IP address changes
The difficulty of encrypting UDP traffic across multiple network paths
The complexity of implementing flow control over unreliable datagram service
The overhead of establishing separate TLS and TCP handshake sequences
explanation: QUIC uses connection IDs instead of IP/port tuples to identify connections, allowing seamless migration when a client's IP address changes (e.g., switching from WiFi to cellular).

text: In TCP, what is the purpose of the Karn's algorithm?
options: It prevents RTT estimation from using retransmitted segments' measurements
It selects the best MSS value based on the path MTU discovery results
It calculates optimal initial window size based on network bandwidth estimates
It determines when to switch from slow start to congestion avoidance phase
explanation: Karn's algorithm addresses the retransmission ambiguity problem by not using RTT measurements from retransmitted segments, since it's unclear if the ACK corresponds to the original or retransmitted segment.

text: What is the BBR (Bottleneck Bandwidth and Round-trip) congestion control approach?
options: A delay-based algorithm that reduces rate only when latency exceeds a threshold
A model-based algorithm that estimates bottleneck bandwidth and minimum RTT
A loss-based algorithm that halves the window on any detected packet loss
A hybrid algorithm combining slow start with multiplicative decrease on timeout
explanation: BBR is a congestion control algorithm developed by Google that builds a model of the network by estimating bottleneck bandwidth and minimum RTT, operating at the optimal point rather than filling buffers.

text: What problem does TCP's Nagle algorithm interaction with delayed ACK cause?
options: It causes excessive retransmissions due to premature timeouts on the sender
It causes connection resets when both algorithms activate simultaneously
It leads to buffer overflow on the receiver due to rapid small packet arrival
It creates a latency problem where small writes are delayed up to 500ms
explanation: When Nagle's algorithm (buffering small segments) interacts with delayed ACK (waiting before sending ACKs), it can create up to 500ms latency for small writes because each waits for the other.

text: What is the purpose of the SCTP (Stream Control Transmission Protocol)?
options: To reduce TCP header size for low-bandwidth satellite communication links
To encrypt all transport layer communications without needing TLS overhead
To provide multi-homing and multi-streaming over a single transport association
To replace UDP entirely for all real-time multimedia streaming applications
explanation: SCTP provides multi-homing (multiple IP addresses per endpoint for failover) and multi-streaming (multiple independent streams within one association), originally designed for telephony signaling.

text: How does Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN) reduce packet loss?
options: It allows routers to signal congestion by marking packets rather than dropping them
It encrypts congestion signals to prevent third parties from dropping packets
It reserves bandwidth for critical flows to prevent congestion from occurring
It duplicates packets across alternate paths to bypass congested network links
explanation: ECN allows routers to mark IP packets (setting CE bits) to signal incipient congestion instead of dropping them, enabling the sender to reduce its rate before packet loss occurs.

text: What is the difference between TCP Tahoe and TCP NewReno in handling multiple losses?
options: NewReno doubles the ssthresh on loss while Tahoe halves it before entering slow start
Tahoe enters fast recovery for each loss while NewReno resets to slow start on every loss
NewReno can handle multiple losses in one window without exiting fast recovery while Tahoe cannot
Tahoe uses selective acknowledgments while NewReno relies only on cumulative acknowledgments
explanation: TCP NewReno improves on Tahoe by staying in fast recovery and detecting additional losses through partial ACKs, retransmitting them without returning to slow start for each loss in the same window.

text: What is a web browser used for in networking?
options: To monitor bandwidth usage across all links
To access and display web pages from servers
To manage database records on the network
To configure router settings and interfaces
explanation: A web browser is a client application used to access, retrieve, and display web pages and resources from web servers using protocols like HTTP and HTTPS.

text: What port does POP3 use for email retrieval?
options: Port 143 for email message retrieval
Port 993 for email message retrieval
Port 25 for email message retrieval
Port 110 for email message retrieval
explanation: POP3 (Post Office Protocol version 3) uses port 110 to retrieve emails from a mail server and download them to the client device.

text: What is the purpose of TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol)?
options: To securely transfer files using encrypted communication channels
To transfer large files with error correction and authentication
To provide simple file transfers without authentication features
To synchronize file versions across distributed storage systems
explanation: TFTP is a simplified file transfer protocol that uses UDP and provides basic file transfer without authentication, used commonly for booting diskless workstations and firmware updates.

text: What does a DNS A record store?
options: The IPv4 address mapped to a domain name
The alias or canonical name for a domain
The mail server address for the domain
The authoritative nameserver for the domain
explanation: A DNS A (Address) record maps a domain name to its corresponding IPv4 address, enabling browsers and applications to find the server hosting a website.

text: What is the role of an email client application?
options: It routes email traffic between different mail servers
It converts email protocols from SMTP to HTTP format
It filters spam at the server before delivery to users
It allows users to compose, send, and read email messages
explanation: An email client (like Outlook or Thunderbird) is software that allows users to compose, send, receive, and manage their email messages.

text: What does the HTTP GET method request from a server?
options: It requests to create a new resource on the server
It requests to retrieve a resource from the server
It requests to update an existing resource on server
It requests to delete a resource from the server
explanation: The HTTP GET method requests the retrieval of a specified resource (like a web page) from the server without modifying it.

text: What is IMAP used for in email communication?
options: Accessing and managing email directly on the server
Sending email messages between mail servers
Filtering incoming email for spam and viruses
Encrypting email content during transmission
explanation: IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol) allows users to access and manage their email directly on the mail server, keeping messages synchronized across multiple devices.

text: What type of DNS record is used for email routing?
options: A PTR record for reverse DNS mail lookups
An A record pointing to the mail server IP
A CNAME record aliasing the mail server name
An MX record specifying the mail exchange server
explanation: An MX (Mail Exchange) record specifies which mail server is responsible for receiving email on behalf of a domain, directing email routing.

text: What does NTP (Network Time Protocol) do?
options: It transfers files between networked computers
It translates network addresses between formats
It synchronizes clocks across networked devices
It monitors network traffic for security threats
explanation: NTP synchronizes the clocks of computers and devices across a network to a reference time source, ensuring consistent and accurate timekeeping.

text: What is the function of a proxy server?
options: It assigns MAC addresses to new network devices
It acts as an intermediary between clients and servers
It generates encryption keys for secure connections
It directly connects two LANs using physical cables
explanation: A proxy server acts as an intermediary that forwards requests between clients and servers, providing functions like caching, filtering, and anonymity.

text: What is the difference between iterative and recursive DNS queries?
options: In iterative, the server resolves the query completely; in recursive, it returns a referral
In recursive, the server resolves the query completely; in iterative, it returns a referral
Recursive queries use TCP while iterative queries always use UDP protocol
Iterative queries bypass caching while recursive queries always check cache first
explanation: In a recursive query, the DNS server takes responsibility for fully resolving the query. In an iterative query, the server returns the best answer it has (often a referral to another server).

text: What HTTP status code range indicates a client error?
options: 300-399 redirection response code range
100-199 informational response codes
400-499 client error response code range
200-299 successful response code range
explanation: HTTP status codes in the 400-499 range indicate client errors, such as 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, and 404 Not Found.

text: What is the purpose of HTTP cookies?
options: To compress web page content for faster loading times
To encrypt communication between browser and web server
To validate the digital certificate of a web server
To store state information for the stateless HTTP protocol
explanation: HTTP cookies store small pieces of state information on the client side, enabling servers to maintain session data, user preferences, and tracking across the inherently stateless HTTP protocol.

text: What is the DNS TTL (Time-to-Live) field used for?
options: To specify how long a DNS record can be cached before refresh
To set the maximum size of a DNS response message packet
To limit the number of hops a DNS query can traverse
To determine the priority of DNS servers for a domain
explanation: The DNS TTL specifies the maximum time (in seconds) that a DNS record can be cached by resolvers before it must be refreshed from the authoritative server.

text: What is the purpose of the HTTP PUT method?
options: To delete the resource located at the specified URI
To retrieve a resource from the specified server URI
To replace the entire resource at the specified URI
To append data to an existing resource on the server
explanation: The HTTP PUT method replaces the entire resource at the specified URI with the request payload, or creates it if it doesn't exist. It is idempotent.

text: What is the CNAME record used for in DNS?
options: To create an alias that points to another domain name
To specify the text-based description of a DNS zone
To map a domain name to an IPv6 address for hosts
To define the service location for specific protocols
explanation: A CNAME (Canonical Name) record creates an alias for a domain name, pointing it to another domain name (the canonical name), which then resolves to an IP address.

text: What is the difference between SFTP and FTPS?
options: FTPS is faster than SFTP because it has less encryption overhead
SFTP runs over SSH while FTPS adds TLS/SSL to the FTP protocol
FTPS runs over SSH while SFTP adds TLS/SSL to the FTP protocol
SFTP uses port 990 while FTPS uses port 22 for connections
explanation: SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol) operates over SSH on port 22 as a completely different protocol, while FTPS extends the existing FTP protocol by adding TLS/SSL encryption.

text: What is the purpose of the HTTP Content-Type header?
options: It defines the maximum size of the request or response payload
It specifies the encoding used to compress the response body
It authenticates the client making the HTTP request to the server
It indicates the media type of the resource being sent or received
explanation: The Content-Type header indicates the media type (MIME type) of the resource, such as text/html, application/json, or image/png, helping the receiver process the content correctly.

text: What is the role of a DNS root server in the name resolution hierarchy?
options: It assigns new domain names to registrars for public sale
It caches resolved DNS entries for all ISP customers worldwide
It directs queries to the appropriate top-level domain servers
It stores all DNS records for every domain on the internet
explanation: DNS root servers are at the top of the DNS hierarchy and direct queries to the appropriate top-level domain (TLD) servers (.com, .org, .net, etc.) for further resolution.

text: What is the HTTP 301 status code used to indicate?
options: The requested resource was temporarily unavailable for maintenance
The requested resource has been permanently moved to a new URL
The server encountered an internal error processing the request
The client must authenticate before accessing the resource
explanation: HTTP 301 indicates a permanent redirect, meaning the requested resource has been permanently moved to a new URL, and clients should use the new URL for future requests.

text: What is HTTP/3's primary underlying transport protocol and why was it chosen?
options: QUIC was chosen because it eliminates head-of-line blocking at the transport layer
TCP was chosen because it provides built-in reliable ordered delivery
DCCP was chosen because it provides congestion control without reliability overhead
SCTP was chosen because it supports multi-homing natively across interfaces
explanation: HTTP/3 uses QUIC (built on UDP) because QUIC eliminates TCP's head-of-line blocking by allowing independent streams, integrates TLS 1.3, and provides faster connection establishment.

text: What is the purpose of the DNS SRV record?
options: It maps domain names to IPv4 addresses for web server access
It creates an alias pointing one domain name to another domain
It stores arbitrary text data associated with a DNS domain name
It specifies the host and port for a specific service within a domain
explanation: DNS SRV records specify the hostname, port, priority, and weight for a particular service (like SIP, LDAP, or XMPP), enabling service discovery within a domain.

text: What is the key difference between HTTP/2 server push and WebSocket communication?
options: WebSocket allows the server to proactively send resources while server push provides full-duplex channels
WebSocket requires HTTP/2 as a transport while server push works independently of version
Server push allows the server to proactively send resources while WebSocket provides full-duplex communication
Server push works only with GET requests while WebSocket works with all HTTP methods
explanation: HTTP/2 server push allows the server to proactively send anticipated resources before the client requests them. WebSocket provides a persistent full-duplex communication channel for real-time bidirectional data exchange.

text: What is the OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) used for in HTTPS?
options: To negotiate the cipher suite for TLS encrypted connections
To check the revocation status of a digital certificate in real time
